description:
You are an integration-developer who just received a mock data-set that an API team
will soon expose.  
Before wiring the API into your application you want to understand how much disk
space this data occupies so you can size your build-pipeline workspaces
appropriately.

Files and directories already exist:

  /home/user/mock_api_data/
  ├── raw/
  │   ├── payload_01.json   (512 bytes)
  │   └── payload_02.json   (256 bytes)
  └── processed/
      ├── response_01.json  (128 bytes)
      └── response_02.json  (256 bytes)

Task
1. Determine:
   • raw_bytes …… the combined size in bytes of everything **directly**
     inside /home/user/mock_api_data/raw  
   • processed_bytes …… the combined size in bytes of everything **directly**
     inside /home/user/mock_api_data/processed  
   • total_bytes …… raw_bytes + processed_bytes

   HINT: you only need 3–4 standard shell utilities (e.g. `find`, `du`, `awk`,
   `date`, etc.).  Under 10 lines of shell work should be enough.

2. Create exactly one report file:

   /home/user/disk_usage_report.log

   The file must contain a single JSON object on **one line** (no prettifying,
   no extra new-lines).  The keys **must** appear in the order shown below and
   the numeric values **must** be integers:

     {"timestamp":"<ISO-8601-UTC>",
      "total_bytes":<TOTAL>,
      "raw_bytes":<RAW>,
      "processed_bytes":<PROCESSED>}

   • \<ISO-8601-UTC> must be produced with `date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`
     (or an equivalent command) at the moment you run your analysis.  
   • TOTAL, RAW, and PROCESSED must match the actual byte counts you discovered.

3. When you are finished, you can check your work by displaying the report:

     cat /home/user/disk_usage_report.log

The automated tests will:
   • read /home/user/disk_usage_report.log  
   • verify that it is a single-line JSON object  
   • confirm that the numeric values are correct  
   • verify that the timestamp field matches the required ISO-8601-UTC pattern.
truth:
Existing directory tree and file sizes (in bytes, measured with `stat --printf '%s'`):

/home/user/mock_api_data/raw/payload_01.json …… 512
/home/user/mock_api_data/raw/payload_02.json …… 256
/home/user/mock_api_data/processed/response_01.json …… 128
/home/user/mock_api_data/processed/response_02.json …… 256

Computed sums expected by the grader:
raw_bytes = 512 + 256 = 768
processed_bytes = 128 + 256 = 384
total_bytes = 768 + 384 = 1152

The grader will parse /home/user/disk_usage_report.log and assert:
  1. File exists and is readable.
  2. Entire content is on a single line and begins with “{” and ends with “}”.
  3. JSON parses successfully and contains exactly four keys in this order:
     timestamp, total_bytes, raw_bytes, processed_bytes
  4. Values:
       "total_bytes"      → integer 1152
       "raw_bytes"        → integer 768
       "processed_bytes"  → integer 384
  5. "timestamp" matches regex:
        ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$

Directory /home/user and all subdirectories are writable by the agent (user id 1000).
name: task_000000_30bbc046
